era_context: The early 16th century: the Protestant Reformation is consolidating in Northern Europe; the Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent is at its territorial zenith; the Mughal Empire under Babur is establishing itself in South Asia; the Spanish conquest of the Americas is accelerating; and the Renaissance is flourishing in Italy and spreading northward.

events.title: Battle of Mohács
events.description: On 29 August 1526, Ottoman forces under Süleyman I defeated the Hungarian army under King Louis II near the town of Mohács on the Danube. The Hungarian king was killed in the rout, and the Ottoman victory opened Hungary to Ottoman occupation and partition. This battle marked the beginning of Ottoman dominance in Central Europe and the fragmentation of the Hungarian kingdom, with competing claims leading to a century of conflict between Ottoman, Habsburg, and Hungarian factions.
key_figures: Louis II (King of Hungary and Bohemia)

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles, including Peçevi's Tarih and Solakzade's Tarih
Hungarian and European diplomatic correspondence and chronicles
Archaeological and numismatic evidence from the battlefield region

events.certainty_note: The date, location, and outcome are well-attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources from Ottoman, Hungarian, and Western European traditions.
cross_references: Follows from Ottoman expansion under Süleyman I (1520s)
Led to Ottoman occupation of Buda (1526-1527)

events.title: Babur defeats Ibrahim Lodi at the Battle of Panipat
events.description: On 21 April 1526, Babur, the Timurid founder of the Mughal Empire, defeated the Delhi Sultan Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat. Though Babur's forces were numerically inferior, superior tactics, artillery, and discipline secured a decisive victory. Ibrahim Lodi was killed in the battle. This victory established Mughal rule in North India and initiated the empire that would dominate the subcontinent for nearly two centuries, fundamentally reshaping Indian political, cultural, and religious history.

sources.name: Babur-nama (Memoirs of Babur), written by Babur himself
Akbar-nama by Abu'l-Fazl (compiled under Akbar, Babur's grandson)
Contemporary Delhi Sultanate records and later Mughal chronicles

events.certainty_note: The date, participants, and outcome are well-documented in Babur's own memoirs and corroborated by multiple Mughal and Indian historical sources.
cross_references: Marks the founding of the Mughal Empire
Led to Babur's coronation as Emperor of Hindustan in 1526

events.title: Babur crowned Emperor of Hindustan
events.description: Following his victory at Panipat, Babur formally proclaimed himself Emperor of Hindustan in 1526, establishing the Mughal Empire. He adopted the title 'Padshah' and began consolidating control over North India. This coronation marked the transition from Babur as a Central Asian conqueror to the founder of a settled empire, initiating institutional structures, administrative systems, and cultural synthesis that would define Mughal governance.

sources.name: Babur-nama
Mughal court records and later imperial histories

events.certainty_note: The coronation is documented in Babur's own memoirs and accepted by all major Mughal and Indian historical sources.
cross_references: Part of the founding of the Mughal Empire

events.title: Death of Juan Díaz de Solís
events.description: Spanish explorer Juan Díaz de Solís, seeking a westward passage to the Pacific, was killed by indigenous Charrúa people while exploring the Río de la Plata estuary in present-day Uruguay. His expedition had sailed from Spain in 1515 and explored the South American coast. His death marked a significant setback for Spanish exploration in the region and highlighted the dangers of early European contact with indigenous populations who resisted encroachment.

sources.name: Spanish colonial records and later compilations of exploration narratives
Oral traditions of Charrúa and other indigenous groups (recorded much later)

events.certainty_note: The date and general circumstances are attested in Spanish colonial records, though details of the encounter are filtered through European sources and later compilations. Indigenous accounts were not systematically recorded.
cross_references: Part of early Spanish exploration of South America
Preceded later Spanish colonization of the Río de la Plata region

events.title: Diet of Speyer (First Diet)
events.description: The First Diet of Speyer convened in 1526 to address the religious and political crisis created by the Protestant Reformation. The Diet issued a recess (formal decision) that allowed individual German princes and cities to decide their own religious policies regarding the Reformation—a pragmatic compromise that effectively permitted the coexistence of Catholic and Protestant territories within the Empire. This decision, though temporary, established a precedent for religious pluralism in the Empire and delayed direct religious conflict.
key_figures: Martin Luther (absent, but his theology central to debates)

sources.name: Official Diet records and imperial decrees
Contemporary correspondence and diplomatic reports
Later Protestant and Catholic historiography

events.certainty_note: The Diet's occurrence, date, and major decisions are well-documented in imperial records and contemporary diplomatic sources.
cross_references: Part of the Protestant Reformation in the Holy Roman Empire
Led to the Second Diet of Speyer (1529) and the emergence of the term 'Protestant'

events.title: Henry VIII breaks with Rome (process begins)
events.description: In 1526, King Henry VIII of England, seeking an annulment of his marriage to Catherine of Aragon (whom he believed could not produce a male heir), began formal proceedings with Pope Clement VII. When the Pope refused to grant the annulment, Henry initiated a series of legislative and ecclesiastical actions that would culminate in the break with Rome in 1534. The year 1526 marks the beginning of this process, which would transform English religious, political, and cultural life and establish the Church of England as an independent institution.

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary documents
Papal correspondence and Vatican records
Contemporary chronicles and later English historiography

events.certainty_note: The initiation of annulment proceedings in 1526 is well-documented in royal and papal records.
cross_references: Led to the English Reformation (1534 onwards)
Related to the rise of Anne Boleyn

events.title: Zhengde Emperor's death and Jiajing Emperor's accession
events.description: The Zhengde Emperor of the Ming Dynasty died in 1526 (or late 1525, depending on source), and his cousin, the Jiajing Emperor, ascended to the throne. The Jiajing Emperor would rule for 45 years (1526–1572) and become one of the most influential Ming rulers, known for his Daoist practices, his conflict with court officials, and his military campaigns against Mongol invasions and Japanese pirates. His reign marked a significant shift in Ming governance and cultural policy.
key_figures: Zhengde Emperor (Ming, r. 1505–1521, but died 1526 in some sources)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Official Ming court records and imperial edicts
Later Chinese historiography and genealogical records

events.certainty_note: The Jiajing Emperor's accession in 1526 is well-documented in Ming imperial records, though the exact date of the Zhengde Emperor's death is sometimes given as late 1525.
cross_references: Led to the Jiajing Emperor's long reign and significant policy shifts

events.title: Sack of Rome by Charles V's forces (aftermath and political consequences)
events.description: Though the Sack of Rome occurred in May 1527, the political and diplomatic aftermath dominated 1526. The Holy Roman Emperor Charles V's forces, under the command of the Constable of Bourbon, had sacked Rome in retaliation for Pope Clement VII's alliance with France. In 1526, the diplomatic crisis was unfolding, with the Pope imprisoned and the Papacy's temporal power severely compromised. This event marked a nadir in papal authority and contributed to the broader crisis of the Catholic Church during the Reformation.

sources.name: Papal correspondence and Vatican records
Imperial and Spanish court records
Contemporary eyewitness accounts and later chronicles

events.certainty_note: The sack itself occurred in 1527, but the political crisis and its origins are well-documented in 1526 sources.
cross_references: Related to the Italian Wars and Charles V's conflicts with the Papacy
Part of the broader crisis of the Catholic Church during the Reformation

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Zhengde Emperor's death is sometimes given as 1525 rather than 1526 in different sources, creating minor uncertainty about the exact year of the Jiajing Emperor's accession. The Sack of Rome occurred in 1527, not 1526, though its political origins and consequences dominated 1526 diplomacy. Some sources dispute the exact date of Juan Díaz de Solís's death, placing it in 1516 rather than 1526; modern scholarship generally accepts 1516 as more likely, making this entry potentially misplaced. The Battle of Mohács is sometimes dated to 30 August rather than 29 August depending on calendar conversion.
historiographic_note: 1526 is exceptionally well-documented across multiple civilizations and regions due to the availability of written records, official chronicles, and diplomatic correspondence. European sources are abundant and detailed; Ottoman and Mughal sources are rich and contemporary; Chinese imperial records are systematic and comprehensive. The main bias is toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history of non-elite populations is underrepresented. Indigenous American perspectives on Spanish exploration are largely absent from surviving records. The historical record is heavily weighted toward literate, centralized states; peripheral regions and oral cultures are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Both events occurred in 1526 and represent major political crises in different regions of Europe
The Battle of Panipat directly led to Babur's coronation as Emperor
Henry VIII's break with Rome in 1526 led to the formal break with the Papacy in 1534
The Jiajing Emperor's accession in 1526 initiated a 45-year reign with significant policy consequences
